title: S3 Objects, Keys, Listings, and Runtime Shapes · Guided Contract Studio
summary: A TypeScript pairing room incrementally builds typed S3 object-model ledger, validates one changed runtime input, and keeps earlier HTTP, accessibility, security, stream, AbortSignal, and resource-budget rules active. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of typed S3 object-model ledger while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, distinguish bucket, key, version ID, delete marker, upload ID, part number, and application identity.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, design opaque or structured keys with explicit delimiter, normalization, tenant, cardinality, migration, and disclosure rules.
In a strict Node 24 and TypeScript 7 service, choose system metadata, user metadata, object tags, database metadata, and event payloads by mutability, limits, query, trust, and lifecycle needs.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Bucket Key Version Identity
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop TypeScript file-delivery team handles case 9272 during a AWS SDK v3 dependency canary. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to separate runtime-valid object key, version, pagination, and strong-read evidence; separate deterministic browser evidence from controlled Node and AWS transfer gates; then change region, retrieval, or transfer cost and defend the revised budget action. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. Node and AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, PostgreSQL, credentials, host state, handle and stream checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?
